title: What Tic TOC Does and Why You Spend Two Hours Here First

segments.label: 01 / 12 · HOOK
title: THE MOST EXPENSIVE MISTAKE
HAPPENS BEFORE YOU WRITE.
segments.body_html:
Outline written→50K words drafted→Architecture breaks

The error is invisible at the time. It becomes visible fifty thousand words later, when a chapter refuses to connect to the one before it.

beats.text: Most authors make their most expensive mistake before a single chapter is drafted. It happens in the planning phase, when you decide what the book is about, who it is for, and what a reader should be able to do after finishing. The mistake is subtle. It is invisible at the time. But it becomes visible fifty thousand words later, when a chapter refuses to connect to the one before it, when the exercises feel decorative, when a colleague reads the manuscript and says, I'm not sure what I'm supposed to do with this. The problem is not the author's writing. The problem is the architecture. And the architecture was decided before any writing happened at all.

segments.label: 02 / 12 · THE STAKES
title: TIC TOC MAKES THAT MISTAKE
CHEAP TO CATCH EARLY.
segments.body_html:
Key fact: The purpose of Tic TOC is to make architectural mistakes expensive to commit and cheap to catch.

By the time you have written fifty thousand words, the cost of discovering a broken architecture is prohibitive. Tic TOC shifts that cost to the planning phase, where it belongs.

beats.text: The purpose of Tic TOC is simple: make that mistake expensive to commit, and cheap to catch. If you catch the architectural error early, before you have written fifty thousand words, the cost of fixing it is negligible. You revise a planning document, not a half-finished manuscript. The economics of authorship demand that the expensive work happen first. Tic TOC enforces this. It forces you to make decisions about what the book is, who it serves, and what it teaches before you draft a single chapter. The TIKTOC.md is a document that makes those decisions visible, integrated, and testable against one another. That is why you spend two hours building it before you write anything else.

segments.label: 03 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: TIKTOC.MD IS AN EIGHT-PART
INTEGRATED DOCUMENT.
segments.body_html:
Part 1Book Concept and Thesis — what the book teaches and why it matters
Part 2Learner Profile — who reads this, what they know, what they believe
Parts 3–6Deployment, positioning, learning arc, prerequisites — the structure
Part 7Learning Outcomes by Chapter — what readers can do, per chapter
Part 8Chapter-by-Chapter TOC — capability statements and bridges

beats.text: Tic TOC is not an outline in the traditional sense. It is an integrated document with eight parts. The first part establishes the book concept and the central thesis—one sentence that states what the book claims. The second part describes the learner: who they are, what knowledge they bring, what misconceptions they carry. Parts three through six define the deployment strategy, the book's position in its field, the three-act learning arc, and the prerequisite map. Part seven is the learning outcomes table, which assigns every chapter a Bloom's ceiling—the maximum cognitive level that chapter needs to reach. Part eight is the chapter-by-chapter breakdown, with each chapter's one-line thesis, opening scenario, and bridge to the next chapter. Together, these parts form a complete architecture. Every decision constrains and informs every other decision.

segments.label: 04 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: PART 1: DEFINE THE BOOK
AS A THESIS, NOT A TOPIC.
segments.body_html:
Topic approachThis book is about AI for designers
Thesis approachAI fluency costs the account unless you protect decisions that carry client trust

The logline answers the question: what changes if someone reads this book? The one-sentence thesis tells you what you are arguing, not what you are describing.

beats.text: Part 1 of TIKTOC.md establishes the book concept and thesis. This is where you answer the question: what is the book actually claiming? The book concept summary explains what the reader will be able to do after finishing it—not what they will be aware of, but what moves they will be able to make. The one-sentence logline distills the book into a single claim: the fluency trap costs you the account; the AI+1 designer keeps it. This is not a topic. This is an argument. The central thesis follows: this book argues that the working freelance designer's most valuable asset is tacit client knowledge that no AI tool can reach. The thesis is the architectural spine. Every chapter must serve it. If a chapter does not serve the thesis, it does not belong in the book.

segments.label: 05 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: PART 2: NAME THE READER'S
MISCONCEPTIONS EXPLICITLY.
segments.body_html:
Misconception 1If the client likes it, it worked
Misconception 2The fluency trap means bad AI output
Misconception 3AI fluency means better prompting
Misconception 4If I reject AI, my role is safe

These are not the reader's stated beliefs. They are invisible assumptions that will block understanding. Naming them forces you to teach against them.

beats.text: Part 2 defines the learner profile. This is more than demographics. It names the reader's prior knowledge—they have working knowledge of Adobe or Figma, they have used ChatGPT or Claude, they have client-facing experience. It names what they do not know—they do not have legal training, they do not understand copyright law, they do not have AI literacy beyond consumer tools. But the critical move is listing prior misconceptions. These are not the reader's stated beliefs. These are the invisible assumptions that block understanding. If I reject AI, my role is safe. The fluency trap means bad AI output. These assumptions will survive your arguments unless you teach against them explicitly. The misconception list forces you to structure the book so that it addresses each misconception head-on. It makes the book's task impossible to hide.

segments.label: 06 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: PART 7: ASSIGN EVERY CHAPTER
A BLOOM'S CEILING.
segments.body_html:
Chapter 1
Evaluate
→
Chapter 5
Create
→
Chapter 7
Create

The ceiling is the maximum cognitive demand that chapter must reach. Not every chapter is a creation chapter. But the book has a cognitive arc, not a flat topical tour.

beats.text: Part 7 assigns each chapter a Bloom's ceiling. This is the maximum cognitive level that chapter needs to reach. Chapter one is evaluate: the reader learns to distinguish and identify. Chapter three is also evaluate. Chapter four is apply: the reader learns to use a framework. Chapter five is create: the reader builds a disclosure framework from scratch. The ceiling matters because it constrains the chapter's scope and claims. If a chapter's ceiling is apply, it should not demand creation-level thinking from the reader. If the ceiling is evaluate, the reader is not expected to build something new; they are expected to assess and distinguish. This assignment forces the book to have a cognitive arc, not just a topical progression. You are not wandering through topics. You are climbing Bloom's taxonomy in a deliberate sequence. The chapters build on one another not just topically but cognitively.

segments.label: 07 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: PART 8: CAPABILITY STATEMENTS
NAME WHAT THE READER CAN DO.
segments.body_html:
Awareness goalReaders will be aware of the delegation map
Capability goalReaders will distinguish the AI+1 designer from the replaced designer

These are action verbs. The reader can identify, distinguish, defend, apply. They are not passive. They test whether the chapter has actually landed.

beats.text: Part 8 translates the book concept into chapter-by-chapter breakdowns. Each chapter gets a one-line thesis: what is this chapter teaching? The critical move is the capability statement. It uses action verbs. The reader learns to distinguish. The reader learns to identify. Not the reader becomes aware of. Not the reader understands. The verbs name what the reader can do after the chapter is finished. Chapter one's outcome: readers learn to distinguish the AI+1 designer from the designer who has been replaced. Chapter five's outcome: readers build a disclosure framework. These are not passive learning goals. They are tests. If a reader finishes chapter one and cannot distinguish, the chapter has failed. This specification makes the chapter's actual success testable. A reader either can or cannot do the thing. Either they pass or they do not.

segments.label: 08 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: BRIDGE QUESTIONS THREAD
CHAPTERS INTO SEQUENCE.
segments.body_html:
Chapter 1 closes→Bridge question→Chapter 2 opens

The bridge from Chapter 1 to 2: The AI+1 designer is not the designer with the most tools. It is the designer with the clearest boundary between assistance and abdication.

beats.text: Part 8 includes bridge questions. These are sentences that connect one chapter's ending to the next chapter's opening. Chapter one closes with a definition of the AI+1 designer. The bridge says: the AI+1 designer is not the designer with the most tools; it is the designer with the clearest boundary between assistance and abdication. Chapter two then turns that frame into a work tool. The bridge is not a transition sentence. It is a logical dependency. It says: now that you understand this, you are ready for the next move. The bridge forces you to make the chapter sequence testable. If the bridge does not work, the chapters are not in the right order, or one of them is teaching the wrong thing. The bridge is how you know that the architecture is coherent. If every bridge holds, the book is a single thread, not a series of disconnected chapters.

segments.label: 09 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: TIC TOC DIFFERS FROM OUTLINES
IN THREE ESSENTIAL WAYS.
segments.body_html:
OutlineLists topics in reading order
Plus notesTopics plus key claims per chapter
Tic TOCBook concept, learner profile, cognitive arc, learning outcomes, chapter bridges — all integrated

Tic TOC is not an outline with extra information. It is an architecture tool. Every decision constrains every other decision.

beats.text: Three things are doing work in Tic TOC that a standard author's outline does not do. First, the capability statements use verbs that name what the reader can do—distinguish, identify—not what they will be aware of. This shifts the book from a knowledge-delivery system to a capability-building system. Second, the Bloom's ceiling table assigns every chapter a maximum cognitive level, which means the book has a cognitive arc, not just a topical progression. You are not wandering through topics; you are climbing a hierarchy of thinking. Third, the bridge question at the end of each chapter preview forces you to make the chapter sequence testable. If the bridge fails, the sequence is wrong. If every bridge holds, the book is coherent. These three elements together—capability specs, cognitive arc, logical bridges—create a document that works like architecture. Every decision constrains and informs the others. You cannot add a chapter without checking whether it breaks the cognitive arc. You cannot change a chapter's outcome without checking whether it breaks the bridge to the next chapter.

segments.label: 10 / 12 · SYNTHESIS
title: THE ARCHITECTURE COMES
BEFORE THE WRITING.
segments.body_html:
Key fact: The most expensive mistake you can make happens before a single chapter is drafted. Architecture determines whether the writing will land.

Tic TOC makes architectural mistakes visible and testable when they are still cheap to fix. Once the chapters are written, the architecture is locked in concrete.

beats.text: Here is the implication that drives the entire Tic TOC system: the architecture comes before the writing, and the architecture is the harder work. You cannot write your way out of a broken architecture. You cannot prose-style a chapter that does not serve the thesis. You cannot edit your way to coherence if the chapters are in the wrong order or if the bridges do not hold. The architecture must be right first. Tic TOC forces you to get the architecture right by making every architectural decision visible, integrated, and testable before you write a single chapter. The learner profile constrains the opening chapter. The thesis constrains every chapter. The cognitive arc constrains the depth each chapter can reach. The capability statements constrain the exercises and the examples. The bridges constrain the sequence. All of these constraints acting together create pressure on the design. That pressure is what makes the work hard. But it is also what makes the work good. The pressure forces you to think clearly about what the book is teaching and who it is teaching it to. The pressure forces coherence.

segments.label: 11 / 12 · THESIS
title: TIC TOC IS HOW YOU BUILD
ARCHITECTURE THAT HOLDS.
segments.body_html:
The working author's most valuable investment is two hours spent clarifying what the book teaches, who reads it, and what they can do after finishing—before any writing happens.

This is not an outline. It is an architecture tool. It makes architectural errors expensive to commit and cheap to catch. And it forces the chapters to be written in service to a thesis, not a topic list.

beats.text: The central claim of this chapter is this: Tic TOC is not a better outline. It is a different kind of tool. It is an architecture tool. It works by making decisions visible, integrated, and testable before any writing happens. The TIKTOC.md you build today will determine whether the chapters you write tomorrow will cohere. It will determine whether the reader's journey makes sense. It will determine whether the exercises feel connected to the thesis or merely decorative. It will determine whether a colleague reading your manuscript says, I see what you're doing, or I'm not sure what I'm supposed to do with this. Tic TOC forces you to think clearly about architecture because it forces every architectural decision to be written down and tested against every other decision. And because the architecture is right before you write, the writing itself becomes simpler. You are not solving architectural problems while writing. You are solving prose problems. You are translating an architecture you already know is sound into chapters that serve it. That is why you spend two hours building Tic TOC before you write anything else.

segments.label: 12 / 12 · CLOSE
title: THE ARCHITECTURE COMES
BEFORE THE WRITING.
segments.body_html:
ARCHITECTURE//CAPABILITY//COHERENCE

AI 1 · Chapter 2 · What Tic TOC Does and Why You Spend Two Hours Here First

beats.text: The architecture comes before the writing. That is the entire argument. Get it right early, when mistakes are cheap. Build a TIKTOC.md that is clear about what the book teaches, who reads it, what they can do, and how the chapters connect. Build it before you write. Make your mistakes there, where they cost nothing. Once the chapters are written, the architecture is locked in. Once a reader has invested time in the book, the coherence is either there or it is not. Tic TOC is how you make sure it is there.
